chunk_summary: This section focuses on pediatric chronic kidney disease (CKD) nutrition, emphasizing growth support, stage-specific electrolyte restrictions, and tailored dietary interventions to manage risks like low protein-energy intake and high phosphate or potassium levels.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of dietary assessment in managing pediatric CKD, including growth monitoring and nutrient intake evaluation.
Identify high-risk dietary patterns in children with CKD, such as low protein-energy intake or reliance on high-sodium foods.
Learn strategies for balancing energy-dense, electrolyte-appropriate diets to support growth in CKD patients.
Recognize the importance of follow-up and reassessment in dietary management for CKD.
key_facts: Energy needs in early CKD are similar to peers but increase with inflammation, dialysis losses, or poor intake.
Protein targets vary by CKD stage and renal replacement therapy; improper protein restriction can worsen wasting.
High-risk patterns include low appetite on dialysis days, reliance on packaged foods (high sodium/phosphate), and low protein-energy intake.
Dietary prescriptions should include energy-dense, electrolyte-appropriate foods like oils, refined cereals, and allowed dairy portions.
Frequent reassessment of potassium, phosphate, and bicarbonate levels is essential to adjust dietary plans.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Nutrition assessment in pediatric CKD must balance growth support with stage-specific electrolyte and fluid restrictions. (Source: Chronic Kidney Disease in Children: An Indian Perspective - PMC)
